title: From Campus to Community: The Role of Higher Education in Strengthening Regional Entrepreneurial Ecosystems in Appalachia
abstract: This study addresses a gap in understanding how small, rural liberal arts higher education institutions (HEIs) the Appalachian College Association Entrepreneurial Alliance (ACAEA) support regional entrepreneurial ecosystems (EEs), and how, if at all, those strategies vary across Regional Commission (ARC) county economic classifications. title: Multimodal Maple Plant Disease Detection Using EfficientNet and Transformer-Based Semantic Fusion
abstract: This paper presents a novel multimodal deep learning framework for maple plant disease detection by integrating visual and semantic information. Traditional systems rely primarily on features extracted from leaf images, which often leads to misclassification in cases of visually similar symptoms. To address this limitation, the proposed approach combines EfficientNet-based convolutional neural networks feature extraction with transformerbased language models, including BERT FLAN-T5, encoding. A Multilayer Perceptron (MLP)-based fusion mechanism is employed integrate textual features, enabling effective cross-modal learning. The model evaluated balanced dataset 2,000 images associated descriptions. Experimental results demonstrate that achieves an accuracy 94.8%, outperforming vision-only text-only models significant margin. Ablation studies comparative analysis confirm effectiveness provides robust scalable solution intelligent has potential applications smart agriculture systems.

title: FD-DDLLM: A large language model enhanced dual-domain transformer for intelligent fault diagnosis
abstract: To overcome the issues of inadequate multi-scale feature representation and limited model interpretability in equipment fault diagnosis under complex operating conditions, this paper proposes a large language enhanced dual-domain transformer for intelligent diagnosis, which integrates time–frequency features with text semantic enhancement. title: Macrophage metabolic reprogramming in sepsis-associated acute lung injury: mechanisms and therapeutic strategies
abstract: Sepsis-associated acute lung injury (S-ALI) remains a life-threatening condition with high mortality and limited therapeutic options. Macrophages, as key sentinels of innate immunity, exhibit remarkable heterogeneity functional plasticity. These properties are fundamentally driven by metabolic reprogramming, which tailors their effector functions to specific microenvironmental demands. Beyond the traditional M1/M2 binary classification, macrophage activation is now appreciated continuous spectrum. Pro-inflammatory macrophages preferentially utilize aerobic glycolysis pentose phosphate pathway, coupled suppressed oxidative phosphorylation (OXPHOS), whereas reparative rely predominantly on OXPHOS fatty acid oxidation (FAO). Key glycolytic enzymes such PFKFB3 PKM2, transcriptional regulator HIF-1α, TCA cycle intermediates including succinate itaconate serve critical checkpoints governing inflammatory responses. During S-ALI, landscape undergoes dynamic temporal shifts: early hyperinflammatory phase characterized enhanced glycolysis, while late immunosuppressive exhibits impaired FAO. This review synthesizes recent advances in understanding how reprogramming orchestrates polarization during encompassing cycle, FAO, amino metabolism. Natural compounds, pharmacological inhibitors, innovative delivery platforms have shown promise metabolism restore immune homeostasis. Notable examples include aerosolized CRISPR/Cas9 nanotherapeutics, biomimetic nanoplatforms, pH-responsive nanoparticles, engineered exosomes. However, challenges broad cytotoxicity, selectivity, incomplete pharmacokinetic characterization, timing intervention evolving septic milieu must be addressed. Future strategies should focus developing cell-type-restricted systems, validating targets human-relevant models, designing phase-specific interventions tailored trajectory S-ALI.

title: Can LLM Agents Respond to Disasters? Benchmarking Heterogeneous Geospatial Reasoning in Emergency Operations
abstract: Operational disaster response goes beyond damage assessment, requiring responders to integrate multi-sensor signals, reason over road networks, populations and key facilities, plan evacuations, produce actionable reports. However, prior work largely isolates remote-sensing perception or evaluates generic tool use, leaving the end-to-end workflows of emergency operations underexplored. In this paper, we introduce Disaster Response Agent benchmark (DORA), first agentic for response: 515 expert-authored tasks across 45 real-world events spanning 10 types, paired with expert-verified, replayable gold trajectories totaling 3,500 tool-call steps. Tasks span five dimensions that cover operational disaster-response pipeline: perception, spatial relational analysis, rescue evacuation planning, temporal evolution reasoning, multi-modal report synthesis. Agents compose calls from a 108-tool MCP library heterogeneous geospatial data: optical, SAR, multi-spectral imagery single-, bi-, multi-temporal sequences (0.015-10m GSD), complemented by elevation social vector layers. We comprehensively evaluate 13 frontier LLMs on our benchmark, revealing three persistent challenges: 1) disaster-domain grounding exposes unique failure modes (damage-semantic grounding, sensor-modality mismatch, disaster-pipeline composition); 2) agents are doubly bottlenecked selection argument where tool-order hints improve accuracy only 1.08-4.40%, alternative scaffolds yield at most 3.24% gain; 3) compositional fragility scales trajectory length, agent-to-gold gap widening 7% 56% long pipelines. DORA establishes rigorous testbed operationally reliable agents.

title: A Composite Activation Function for Learning Stable Binary Representations
abstract: Activation functions play a central role in neural networks by shaping internal representations. Recently, learning binary activation representations has attracted significant attention due to their advantages computational and memory efficiency, as well interpretability. However, training with Heaviside activations remains challenging, non-differentiability obstructs standard gradient-based optimization. In this paper, we propose Heavy Tailed Function (HTAF), smooth approximation the function that enables stable We construct HTAF sigmoid hyperbolic tangent composite theoretically show it maintains large gradient mass around zero inputs while exhibiting slower decay tail regions. Spiking Neural Networks, Binary Networks Deep can be trained stably using Finally, introduce Implicit Concept Bottleneck Models (ICBMs), an interpretable image model leverages induce discrete feature Extensive experiments across various architectures datasets demonstrate ICBM discretization achieving prediction performance comparable or better than models.

title: REALISTA: Realistic Latent Adversarial Attacks that Elicit LLM Hallucinations
abstract: Large language models (LLMs) achieve strong performance across many tasks but remain vulnerable to hallucinations, motivating the need for realistic adversarial prompts that elicit such failures. We formulate hallucination elicitation as a constrained optimization problem, where goal is find semantically coherent are equivalent benign user prompts. Existing methods limited: discrete prompt-based attacks preserve semantic equivalence and coherence search only over limited set of prompt variations, while continuous latent-space explore richer space often decode into no longer valid rephrasings. To address these limitations, we propose REALISTA, attack framework. REALISTA constructs an input-dependent dictionary editing directions, each corresponding rephrasing, optimizes combinations directions in latent space. This design combines flexibility with realism rephrasing-based attacks. Experiments demonstrate achieves superior or comparable state-of-the-art on open-source LLMs and, crucially, succeeds attacking large reasoning under free-form response settings, prior fail. Code available at https://github.com/Buyun-Liang/REALISTA.

title: Agent-Based Post-Hoc Correction of Agricultural Yield Forecasts
abstract: Accurate crop yield forecasting in commercial soft fruit production is constrained by the data available typical farm records, which lack sensor networks, satellite imagery, and high-resolution meteorological inputs that most state-of-the-art approaches assume. We propose a structured LLM agent framework performs post-hoc correction of existing model predictions, encoding agricultural domain knowledge across tools for phase detection, bias learning, range validation. Evaluated on proprietary strawberry dataset public USDA corn harvest dataset, refinement XGBoost reduced MAE 20% MASE 56% strawberry, with consistent improvements Moirai2 (MAE 24%, 22%) Random Forest 28%, 66%) baselines. Using Llama 3.1 8B as produced strongest corrections all configurations; LLaVA 13B showed inconsistent gains, highlighting sensitivity to choice model.
